title: Podman cannot read the default auth file when using the `sudo`
body:
### Issue Description

When using `sudo`, podman failed to read the default auths.

### Steps to reproduce the issue

Steps to reproduce the issue
1. Check the default auth, the image can be pulled successfully **without** the `sudo`
```console
[cloud-user@preserve-olm-env2 jian]$ sudo cat ${XDG_RUNTIME_DIR}/containers/auth.json |jq
{
  "quay.io": {
    "auth": "xxx"
  }
}

[cloud-user@preserve-olm-env2 jian]$ podman pull quay.io/openshift-release-dev/ocp-v4.0-art-dev@sha256:cf9db3bcde9778f347a83564cfbc85a07e6592690184e33afbb077cd01e5103e
Trying to pull quay.io/openshift-release-dev/ocp-v4.0-art-dev@sha256:cf9db3bcde9778f347a83564cfbc85a07e6592690184e33afbb077cd01e5103e...
...
fb9b68e7cfcac23a591fe0a5a3c9bfec267f1f2082fb3524af33587d295d645e
```
2. Pull it **with** the `sudo`


### Describe the results you received

Describe the results you received

### Describe the results you expected

Auth fails with `sudo`.
```console
[cloud-user@preserve-olm-env2 jian]$ sudo podman pull quay.io/openshift-release-dev/ocp-v4.0-art-dev@sha256:cf9db3bcde9778f347a83564cfbc85a07e6592690184e33afbb077cd01e5103e
Trying to pull quay.io/openshift-release-dev/ocp-v4.0-art-dev@sha256:cf9db3bcde9778f347a83564cfbc85a07e6592690184e33afbb077cd01e5103e...
Error: initializing source docker://quay.io/openshift-release-dev/ocp-v4.0-art-dev@sha256:cf9db3bcde9778f347a83564cfbc85a07e6592690184e33afbb077cd01e5103e: reading manifest sha256:cf9db3bcde9778f347a83564cfbc85a07e6592690184e33afbb077cd01e5103e in quay.io/openshift-release-dev/ocp-v4.0-art-dev: unauthorized: access to the requested resource is not authorized
```

comments.body:
If you run it like this `sudo cat ${XDG_RUNTIME_DIR}/containers/auth.json |jq` then the user shell will expend the var before running sudo.
Try `sudo sh -c 'cat ${XDG_RUNTIME_DIR}/containers/auth.json |jq'` then you will see that this file does not exists for root.

Or just run `sudo podman pull --authfile ${XDG_RUNTIME_DIR}/containers/auth.json ...` to make it use the existing file.

```
$ sudo echo $XDG_RUNTIME_DIR 
/run/user/1000
$ sudo sh -c 'echo $XDG_RUNTIME_DIR' 
                       <<<---empty
```

> Try sudo sh -c 'cat ${XDG_RUNTIME_DIR}/containers/auth.json |jq' then you will see that this file does not exists for root.

Yes, thanks! But, as I remember the Podman will read the `Docker` auth config if the `${XDG_RUNTIME_DIR}/containers/auth.json` doesn't exist, but it did not with the `sudo`, as follows, Docker auth did exist.
```console
[cloud-user@preserve-olm-env2 mirror-tmp]$ sudo sh -c 'cat ~/.docker/config.json |jq'
{
  "auths": {
...
```

> Or just run sudo podman pull --authfile ${XDG_RUNTIME_DIR}/containers/auth.json ... to make it use the existing file.

Yes, but it's really inconvenient. I have to specify the auth file via `--authfile` every time. :(

You can use `REGISTRY_AUTH_FILE` env instead of the option.

As for using `.docker/config.json` I don't know, I leave that for @mtrmac or @vrothberg to answer.

`sudo` changes `HOME` to root’s one, so I think does not point to the original users’ home directory either.

(The entire problem space of `su`/`sudo` and using (or overwriting!) configuration with the invoking users’ home directory is just problematic.)

---

I would suggest thinking about why you are mixing `sudo podman pull` with a non-`sudo` `login`, in the first place.

Each user account has its own set of credentials, and tools are designed to make _that_ easy to use (and, if anything, to make it _hard_ to accidentally share credentials across users).

In particular, if this is some kind of automated script, the right solution might be to use _neither_ users’ credentials; create a single-purpose file just for that script, and use `--authfile` with all Podman commands.

Hi @mtrmac , thanks for your suggestions! I have to use the `sudo` for the normal user since issue #19438 . And, I'm curious why the `podman` cannot read the `~/.docker/config.json` when using `sudo`. As the above shows, it did in there.
```console
[cloud-user@preserve-olm-env2 mirror-tmp]$ sudo sh -c 'cat ~/.docker/config.json |jq'
{
  "auths": {
...
```
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.

> And, I'm curious why the `podman` cannot read the `~/.docker/config.json` when using `sudo`.

`podman --log-level=debug pull …` should contain details about where the code was looking for credentials, and where they were found.
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
Since we never heard back, closing.
